docs.title: AI Safe Charity Index
docs.text:
Practical, evidence-led guidance to help UK charities use AI safely, confidently and responsibly.

The aim is simple: help charities save time with AI without risking people's data, trust or safety.

docs.title: Start With the Core Check
docs.text:
Use the Before You Paste framework before putting information into an AI tool.

It helps you decide whether a task is:

  • Green: go ahead with normal checks
  • Amber: pause and check
  • Red: stop

docs.title: What This Site Will Cover
docs.text:
• safer use of AI in everyday charity work
  • GDPR and data-protection basics
  • safeguarding considerations
  • trustee and governance guidance
  • common AI tool guidance
  • templates, checklists and review processes
  • updates, evidence and release notes

docs.title: Current Status
docs.text: This site is in its foundation build. Pages marked as draft are being converted from the project specification corpus and should be reviewed before being treated as final guidance.

docs.title: Templates
docs.text: This section will include reusable AI policy, staff rules, trustee briefing, approved-tools register, AI-use register, tool-assessment template, output-review checklist, incident form and DPIA triage resources.

docs.title: About
docs.text:
AI Safe Charity Index is an open, living resource for charities that want to use artificial intelligence responsibly.

It translates complex guidance into practical steps that charity staff, volunteers, managers and trustees can understand and use.

The project is guided by evidence, plain English, charity relevance, accessibility and human accountability.

docs.title: Creator
docs.text:
AI Safe Charity Index was originally created by Matthew James Nel.

Connect with Matthew on LinkedIn

docs.title: Open Collaboration
docs.text:
The project invites constructive edits from collaborators who can improve the guidance, sources, accessibility, plain-English content, templates or charity relevance.

Researchers, practitioners, trustees, charity staff, data-protection professionals and safeguarding specialists are also invited to contribute evidence and research on AI policy in charities.

See the Contact page for collaboration and research topics.

docs.title: Contact
docs.text:
AI Safe Charity Index was originally created by Matthew James Nel.

The project is being developed as an open, evidence-led resource for UK charities, and constructive edits, corrections and research contributions are welcome.

Connect with Matthew on LinkedIn

docs.title: Collaborate on the Knowledge Base
docs.text:
You can help improve the project by:

  • suggesting edits to public guidance
  • flagging unclear or outdated content
  • sharing reliable sources
  • reviewing pages for charity relevance
  • reviewing pages for accessibility and plain English
  • suggesting practical examples for small and medium charities
  • improving templates, checklists and governance tools

Please avoid sharing personal data, safeguarding information, confidential charity material, passwords, API keys or private case details.

docs.title: Research Collaboration
docs.text:
Matthew welcomes conversations with people interested in research on:

  • AI policy in charities
  • responsible AI governance for small and medium charities
  • trustee oversight of AI
  • charity staff confidence and training needs
  • AI, safeguarding and service delivery
  • AI and data protection in practical charity workflows
  • tool adoption, risk assessment and approved-tools processes

The project is especially interested in research that can help charities make safer, clearer and more proportionate decisions about AI.

docs.title: Corrections and Feedback
docs.text:
If you spot an error or want to suggest a source, please include:

  • the page title or URL
  • what should change
  • the source or evidence supporting the change
  • whether the issue is urgent or high risk

Do not use GitHub issues or public project spaces to share personal data, safeguarding information, confidential charity material, API keys or passwords.

docs.title: FAQ
docs.text: This page will answer common questions about the project, AI safety, review dates, evidence standards and contribution routes.

docs.title: Glossary
docs.text: This page will define AI, data-protection, safeguarding and governance terms in plain English.

docs.title: Accessibility
docs.text:
The project aims to meet WCAG 2.2 AA wherever practical.

Accessibility is part of editorial quality, technical quality and public trust. Guidance should use clear headings, descriptive links, plain English, accessible tables, meaningful alt text and text labels that do not rely on colour alone.

Green, Amber and Red guidance must always include text labels and explanations, not colour alone.

docs.title: AI and Data Protection
docs.text:
This section will explain the data-protection concepts charities need when using AI tools.

Planned pages include personal data, special category data, anonymisation, pseudonymisation, lawful basis, data minimisation, DPIAs, automated decision-making and data breaches.

For now, use the Before You Paste framework before entering information into any AI tool.

docs.title: Before You Paste
docs.text:
Before you paste information into an AI tool, stop and check what the information contains, where it may go, what the tool may do with it, and what could happen if the output is wrong.

This framework does not replace professional advice, a Data Protection Impact Assessment, safeguarding procedures, supplier due diligence or formal organisational approval.

docs.title: The Quick Check
docs.text:
Ask these questions in order:

  1. What task am I trying to do?
  2. What information am I planning to enter?
  3. Could a person be identified directly or indirectly?
  4. Does it include personal data?
  5. Does it include higher-risk information?
  6. Is the information confidential?
  7. Has it genuinely been anonymised?
  8. Is the AI tool approved for this use?
  9. Is using this information necessary?
  10. Could the output affect someone?
  11. Could an inaccurate or biased output cause harm?
  12. Who must review or approve the use?

docs.title: Green: Go Ahead With Normal Checks
docs.text:
Green uses are usually low risk. The information is public, fictional, synthetic or genuinely non-personal. The tool is approved for the task, no significant decision about a person is being made, and a person will review the output.

Examples:

  • brainstorming campaign names
  • rewriting already-public website copy
  • summarising a public report
  • creating a meeting agenda
  • generating fictional examples

Green does not mean the output is automatically accurate.

docs.title: Amber: Pause and Check
docs.text:
Amber uses need more thought before you continue.

Pause when the task involves:

  • personal data
  • internal information
  • unpublished documents
  • donor, staff, volunteer or beneficiary information
  • decisions that could affect people
  • unclear tool settings or account terms

Check your charity's policy, speak to the right internal lead, remove unnecessary information, and use an approved route where one exists.

docs.title: Red: Stop
docs.text:
Stop when the information includes:

  • safeguarding case details
  • passwords, API keys or security credentials
  • special category data without an approved arrangement
  • confidential legal, HR or disciplinary material
  • identifiable beneficiary case notes
  • information for an automated decision that could significantly affect someone

Use your charity's established safeguarding, data-protection, legal, HR or senior-management process instead.

docs.title: Safer Alternatives
docs.text:
When in doubt:

  • use a fictional example
  • remove identifiers
  • summarise the issue at a higher level
  • use public information
  • ask for structure or wording help without sharing the real case
  • use an approved internal tool
  • ask a manager, safeguarding lead or data-protection lead

docs.title: Everyday Charity Work
docs.text: This section will organise AI guidance around real charity tasks, including communications, fundraising, service delivery, safeguarding, HR, volunteers, finance, governance, research, meetings and digital work.

docs.title: Governance
docs.text:
Good AI governance should be achievable, proportionate and useful.

This section will cover AI policies, approved tools, AI-use registers, risk registers, trustee briefings, incident processes, maturity models and quarterly review.

docs.title: Learning Paths
docs.text: This section will curate existing guidance for common roles and starting points, such as new AI users, small charity leaders, trustees, fundraisers, communicators, service-delivery teams and data-protection leads.

docs.title: Privacy
docs.text:
This privacy page is a placeholder for the public site.

The project should minimise personal data collection. Public repositories must never contain safeguarding records, identifiable beneficiary data, employee records, confidential organisational information or raw research responses.

docs.title: Research and Community
docs.text:
This section invites research, feedback and practical collaboration on safer AI use in charities.

AI Safe Charity Index was originally created by Matthew James Nel as an open, evidence-led resource for UK charities.

Connect with Matthew on LinkedIn

docs.title: Research Interests
docs.text:
The project welcomes research conversations on:

  • AI policy in charities
  • responsible AI governance for small and medium charities
  • trustee oversight and assurance
  • staff confidence, training and adoption barriers
  • AI use in fundraising, communications, HR, governance and service delivery
  • AI, safeguarding and vulnerable people
  • AI, GDPR and data minimisation in charity workflows
  • approved-tools processes and practical risk assessment

docs.title: Ways to Contribute
docs.text:
You can help by:

  • suggesting sources
  • reviewing draft guidance
  • proposing practical examples
  • sharing anonymised workflow insights
  • testing pages for clarity and accessibility
  • identifying missing topics
  • contributing to research packs

Do not submit personal data, safeguarding case details, confidential charity material or private research responses through public project spaces.

docs.title: Start Here
docs.text:
AI can help charities draft, summarise, plan and learn. It can also create privacy, safeguarding, accuracy and accountability risks when used without clear checks.

Start with three rules:

  1. Do not paste personal, confidential or safeguarding information into an unapproved AI tool.
  2. Use the least information needed for the task.
  3. Keep a human responsible for the output and the final decision.

Recommended first pages:

  • Before You Paste
  • AI and Data Protection
  • Everyday Charity Work
  • Governance

docs.title: Tools
docs.text:
This section will cover tool evaluation and guidance for common AI products, including ChatGPT, Claude, Google Gemini, Microsoft Copilot and Notion AI.

Tool guidance must be checked against current provider documentation before publication.

docs.title: Updates
docs.text:
This section records what changed, what is planned and what still needs review.

  • Changelog
  • Roadmap

Foundation build:

  • MkDocs Material site foundation
  • task-based navigation skeleton
  • Before You Paste starter guidance
  • repository governance files
  • GitHub Pages deployment workflow

Known limitations:

  • most guidance pages are section stubs
  • source and claim registers are not yet published
  • frontmatter validation is not yet automated

docs.title: Phase 1: Foundation
docs.text:
• Create the MkDocs site foundation.
  • Publish the task-based navigation skeleton.
  • Add repository governance files.
  • Publish the first Before You Paste guidance.

docs.title: Phase 2: Core Guidance
docs.text:
• Expand Before You Paste.
  • Add AI and data-protection guidance.
  • Add safeguarding and trustee guidance.
  • Add charity workflow pages.

docs.title: Phase 3: Tools and Templates
docs.text:
• Publish the tool evaluation framework.
  • Add current tool guides.
  • Add policy, register and review templates.

docs.title: Phase 4: Quality Gates
docs.text:
• Add frontmatter validation.
  • Add link checking.
  • Add review-date reporting.
  • Publish evidence and claim registers.
